items.q: What is the difference between a stakeholder and a shareholder?
items.correct: A shareholder owns shares in the company; a stakeholder is anyone affected by or with interest in the business — employees, customers, suppliers, community, plus shareholders.
items.misconception: Stakeholders and shareholders are different spellings of the same word.
items.random_wrong: Stakeholders are managers and shareholders are workers.
items.why: All shareholders are stakeholders, but most stakeholders are not shareholders. Students treat the terms as interchangeable, missing the wider scope of stakeholder analysis vs ownership.

items.correct: The external macro-environment: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal and Environmental factors affecting a business.
items.misconception: PESTLE analyses the internal strengths and weaknesses of a business.
items.random_wrong: PESTLE stands for Profit, Earnings, Sales, Trade, Loans, Equity.
items.why: Students confuse PESTLE (external) with SWOT (internal + external). PESTLE is about the wider environment a business cannot control; SWOT mixes internal and external.

items.q: How is Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) calculated?
items.correct: Operating profit divided by capital employed (total equity + non-current liabilities), expressed as a percentage. It measures how efficiently the business uses its long-term financing to generate profit.
items.misconception: ROCE is just net profit divided by revenue.
items.random_wrong: ROCE is the wages divided by hours worked.
items.why: ROCE compares profit to capital used, not revenue. It distinguishes capital-efficient businesses from revenue-heavy but capital-intensive ones. Students often confuse it with profit margin.
Correct answer: Specific & Timed. “Silly & Memorable” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Why the business exists. “A failed advert” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Goal: sell 200 cakes by July. Sold 150. Percent achieved?
items.why: The real figure is 75. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
